title: Representing Tropical Linear Spaces by Circuits
overall_summary: The paper studies tropical linear spaces through their circuit hyperplane descriptions, with special emphasis on the constant-coefficient case, where tropical linear spaces are Bergman fans of matroids. Its main combinatorial tools are support-based circuit conditions, reduction of tropical-basis equality to 0/1 points and flats, and generation of circuit conditions by pasting circuits. It also recalls Speyer's matroidal-subdivision description of tropical linear spaces and proves a cocircuit parametrization theorem for realizable tropical linear spaces. For the original problem, the most useful content is that containment/equality of Bergman-type tropical spaces can often be tested by circuits and flats, while realizable tropical linear spaces also admit a dual cocircuit/tropical-span presentation that may interact with order-reversing dualities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Circuit description of tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the tropical semiring R∪{∞} with tropical addition given by minimum and tropical multiplication given by ordinary addition, and let TP^{n-1} be tropical projective space. A point p=(p_B) indexed by d-subsets B of [n] is a tropical Plücker vector if, for every (d−2)-subset S of [n] and every four distinct i,j,k,l outside S, the minimum of p_{Sij}+p_{Skl}, p_{Sik}+p_{Sjl}, and p_{Sil}+p_{Sjk} is attained at least twice. For each (d+1)-subset I of [n], define the associated circuit tropical linear form h_I(x)=min_{i in I}(p_{I minus {i}}+x_i). The tropical linear space associated to p is the set of x in TP^{n-1} such that, for every I, the minimum in h_I(x) is attained at least twice. In the constant-coefficient case, where all coefficients are 0 or ∞, these tropical hyperplanes depend only on their finite supports; those supports are the circuits of a matroid M, and the tropical linear space is the Bergman fan or tropical variety T(C)=intersection over circuits C of M of the sets of x such that min{x_i : i in C} is attained at least twice.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the setup recalled in the introduction and Section 2. The only point emphasized by the paper is that when coefficients are only 0 and ∞, adding the finite coefficients does not change any comparisons except by deleting ∞ entries, so each tropical hyperplane condition depends solely on the support of the corresponding form. Thus the constant-coefficient theory can be expressed purely in terms of the circuit supports of a matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the direct sum M1⊕M2 of matroids on disjoint ground sets, and let C, C1, and C2 be their respective circuit sets. A subset B of C is a tropical basis for M, meaning that the intersection of tropical hyperplanes T(B) equals the full Bergman fan T(C), if and only if B is the union B1∪B2 of tropical bases B1 for M1 and B2 for M2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The circuits of a direct sum of matroids are exactly the circuits of the summands, viewed in the disjoint union of ground sets. Therefore imposing all circuit hyperplane conditions for M is the same as imposing all circuit conditions for M1 on the first set of coordinates and all circuit conditions for M2 on the second set. If B1 and B2 are tropical bases, their union clearly cuts out the same intersection as all circuits of M. Conversely, if B is a tropical basis for M and its restriction to, say, C1 failed to be a tropical basis for M1, an extra point satisfying the B1-conditions but not all C1-conditions could be extended in the coordinates of M2 to give an extra point for M, contradicting that B is a tropical basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid with circuit set C, and for B⊂C let T(B) be the intersection of the tropical hyperplanes determined by the circuits in B. If T(B)∩{0,1}^n equals T(C)∩{0,1}^n, then T(B)=T(C). Equivalently, in the constant-coefficient case, to verify that a collection B of circuits is a tropical basis it is enough to check equality on 0/1 vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since B⊂C, every full circuit condition contains at least as many hyperplanes as the B-conditions, so T(C)⊂T(B). Suppose T(B) has an extra point x not in T(C). Then some circuit C0 in C∖B excludes x, meaning that m=min{x_i : i in C0} is attained at a unique coordinate of C0. Define a 0/1 vector v by v_i=1 if x_i>m and v_i=0 if x_i≤m. On C0, the minimum of v is still attained uniquely, so v is not in T(C). On the other hand, if a circuit C' in B has its minimum on x attained at least twice, then after thresholding at m the minimum on v over C' is also attained at least twice: either two entries of C' have x-value at most m, or all entries have x-value greater than m, or the original two minimum entries remain tied. Thus v lies in T(B). This produces an extra 0/1 point, proving the contrapositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Flat criterion for 0/1 points, quoted from Ardila-Klivans
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid M with circuit set C, a 0/1 vector v lies in the Bergman fan T(C) if and only if its support supp(v)={i : v_i=1} is a flat of M. Consequently, a collection B of circuits is a tropical basis if and only if the circuit hyperplanes from B exclude every 0/1 vector whose support is not a flat.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Ardila and Klivans for this fact. In circuit language, a 0/1 vector with support F violates the circuit condition for a circuit C exactly when C has precisely one element outside F, because that unique outside element has coordinate 0 and all other elements of C have coordinate 1. The standard circuit characterization of flats says that F is a flat exactly when no circuit has exactly one element outside F. This matches the tropical condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a collection of circuits of a matroid M. Suppose every circuit of M not in S can be obtained by successively pasting circuits in S, where pasting two circuits A and B with a unique common element e means replacing them by their symmetric difference A△B=(A∖B)∪(B∖A), assumed at each step to be a circuit. Then S is a tropical basis for M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to show that if a weight vector x satisfies the tropical hyperplane conditions for two circuits A and B sharing only e, then it also satisfies the condition for C=A△B. The paper checks three cases. First, if A attains its minimum at two elements a,a' different from e and B attains its minimum at two elements b,b' different from e, then C attains its minimum at least twice among whichever of {a,a'} or {b,b'} has the smaller weight. Second, if A has minimum at a and e while B has minimum at b and b' not equal to e, then b and b' have weight no larger than e, while a has the same weight as e, so C has a repeated minimum at b,b' or possibly at more elements. Third, if both A and B attain their minima using e, say at a,e and b,e, then x_a=x_e=x_b, and C has a repeated minimum at a and b. Iterating this argument along the prescribed sequence of pastings shows that all circuit conditions follow from S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the uniform matroid U_{d,n}, whose circuits are all (d+1)-subsets of [n], fix an element i in [n]. Then B={C⊂[n] : |C|=d+1 and i∈C} is an inclusion-minimal tropical basis for U_{d,n}. The paper notes that such bases need not be cardinality-minimal and are not unique in general.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take i=1. By Lemma 3 it is enough to exclude all 0/1 points excluded by some circuit. If a 0/1 point x is excluded by a circuit Cx, then Cx has exactly one coordinate k with x_k=0. If 1 lies in Cx, then Cx is already in B. If 1 does not lie in Cx and x_1=1, replace any element of Cx other than k by 1; the resulting (d+1)-set is a circuit containing 1 and still has k as the unique zero, so it excludes x. If 1 does not lie in Cx and x_1=0, replace k by 1; the resulting circuit contains 1 and now has 1 as its unique zero, so it excludes x. Thus B is a tropical basis. For inclusion-minimality, fix C* in B and define x by x_j=1 for j in C*∖{1} and x_j=0 otherwise. The circuit C* excludes x, while every other circuit in B has at least two zero coordinates on x, so removing C* creates an extra point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the graphic matroid of a graph G with no loops and no parallel edges, with ground set E(G) and circuits equal to the cycles of G. The unique minimal tropical basis for M is the set of induced cycles of G, meaning cycles that are induced subgraphs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For sufficiency, take any non-induced cycle C. The induced subgraph on the vertices of C has a chord, which splits C into two smaller cycles. Repeating this chord decomposition expresses C as a successive pasting of induced cycles. Lemma 4 then implies that the induced cycles impose all cycle conditions. For necessity, let C be an induced cycle and assign weight 1 to all but one edge of C, weight 0 to the remaining edge of C, and weight 0 to every edge not in C. Then C has a unique minimum and is excluded. Any other cycle either avoids the problem or, if it uses edges of C but is not C, must use at least two edges outside C; otherwise C would have a chord and would not be induced. Those outside edges have weight 0, so the other cycle has a repeated minimum. Thus each induced cycle is excluded by a witness point not excluded by any other cycle condition, forcing it to appear in every tropical basis. Since the induced cycles themselves are sufficient, they form the unique minimal tropical basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected 3-edge-connected graph, and let M be its cographic matroid, whose circuits are the inclusion-minimal edge cuts of G. The unique minimal tropical basis for M consists of exactly those edge cuts C such that removing C splits G into two connected subgraphs each of which is 2-edge-connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For sufficiency, if a cut C splits G into connected subgraphs U and V, define the index of C to be the total number of bridges in U and V. Index 0 cuts are precisely those splitting G into two 2-edge-connected subgraphs. The paper proves by induction on the index that any positive-index cut is obtained by pasting lower-index cuts. If e is a bridge in U, splitting U into A and B, let br_A, br_B, and br_V be the numbers of bridges in A, B, and V. Then index(C)=1+br_A+br_B+br_V. Let C' be the cut separating A from B∪V, and let C'' be the cut separating B from A∪V. Because G is 3-edge-connected, there are at least two edges between B and V; otherwise e together with a unique such edge would disconnect G. Hence any bridge in B∪V is already a bridge in B or V, so index(C')≤br_A+br_B+br_V, and similarly index(C'')≤br_A+br_B+br_V. The original cut C is obtained by pasting C' and C'' along e, so induction plus Lemma 4 proves sufficiency. For necessity, suppose C splits G into 2-edge-connected subgraphs U and V. Put weight 0 on all edges inside U and inside V, put weight 0 on one chosen edge of C, and put weight 1 on all other edges of C. Then C is excluded by having a unique minimum. Any other minimal cut C' must disconnect U or V; if it disconnects U into A and B, then because U is 2-edge-connected there are at least two weight-0 edges between A and B in C'. Thus C' has a repeated minimum. Hence every index 0 cut is forced, and the basis is unique minimal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R10 be the regular matroid whose ground set is the edge set of K5 and whose circuits are the fifteen 4-cycles of K5 together with the complements of those 4-cycles. The unique minimal tropical basis of R10 consists of the fifteen 4-cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Necessity of each 4-cycle is shown by a 0/1 witness. For a 4-cycle C={a1,a2,a3,a4}, set x_{a1}=x_{a2}=x_{a3}=1 and set all other coordinates to 0. Then C has a unique minimum, while no other 4-cycle shares three elements with C and every 4-cycle-complement contains enough elements outside C to have a repeated minimum. Thus every 4-cycle must occur in any tropical basis. For sufficiency, Lemma 3 reduces to 0/1 points. If a 0/1 point is excluded by a 4-cycle-complement C={a1,...,a6}, relabel so a1 is the unique zero on C and a2,...,a6 are ones. The paper uses the K5 picture: no 4-cycle-complement contains a 4-cycle, and for any five-element subset of the six edges of such a complement there exists a 4-cycle meeting that subset in exactly three edges. This gives a 4-cycle that also excludes the point. Therefore the complement circuits add no conditions beyond the 4-cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Matroidal subdivision description, recalled from Speyer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ_{d,n} be the hypersimplex, the convex hull in R^n of the 0/1 vectors with coordinate sum d. A vector p indexed by d-subsets of [n] is a tropical Plücker vector if and only if the regular subdivision of Δ_{d,n} induced by p is matroidal, meaning every face is a matroid polytope. The corresponding tropical linear space is the polyhedral subcomplex of the dual subdivision consisting of cells dual to matroid polytopes of loop-free matroids. A coordinate p_B equal to ∞ is equivalent to deleting the corresponding vertex of Δ_{d,n}. In the constant-coefficient case, with all coordinates 0 or ∞, the subdivision contains a single matroid polytope, and the circuits of the associated matroid are the supports of the circuit forms. Because Δ_{d,n} lies in the affine hyperplane with coordinate sum d, the dual complexes and tropical linear spaces have lineality space containing R(1,...,1). Since every cell of a matroidal subdivision is a matroid polytope, the star of any face in a tropical linear space is isomorphic to a constant-coefficient tropical linear space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between tropical Plücker vectors and matroidal subdivisions, and the dual-subcomplex description of the tropical linear space, are recalled from Speyer. The paper then explains the consequences used later: infinite Plücker coordinates remove vertices; 0/∞ heights give the constant-coefficient single-matroid-polytope case; lineality comes from the affine span of the hypersimplex; and local stars are constant-coefficient because locally a dual cell sees the normal fan of a matroid polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Realizable tropical linear spaces and their circuit forms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of Puiseux series with complex coefficients, and let deg:K→T send a nonzero series to its lowest exponent and send 0 to ∞. If L is a d-dimensional linear subspace of K^n with Plücker vector P, then deg(P) is a tropical Plücker vector. When a tropical Plücker vector arises this way, the corresponding tropical linear space coincides with deg(L)⊂T^n and is called realizable. For a linear form f=a_1X_1+...+a_nX_n over K, its tropicalization is min_i(deg(a_i)+x_i). In this realizable setting, the circuit forms defining the tropical linear space are precisely the tropicalizations of linear forms with minimal support in the orthogonal complement L⊥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is recalled in Section 4 as standard background. The Plücker vector of L tropicalizes to a tropical Plücker vector, and the tropical linear space defined by the resulting circuit forms agrees with the coordinatewise degree image of L. Minimal-support linear forms in L⊥ give the classical circuits; tropicalizing them yields exactly the circuit hyperplanes used in the tropical definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be an (n−k)-dimensional linear subspace of K^n, where K is the Puiseux series field, and assume all Plücker coordinates of L are nonzero. Let M be an m by n matrix over K with m≥k whose rows are nonzero elements of L⊥. If the rows of M form a tropical basis for L, meaning that their tropicalizations cut out deg(L), then every choice of k columns of deg(M) has tropical rank k. Here the tropical determinant of a square matrix is the minimum over permutation sums, and tropical rank k for an m by k matrix means that it contains a k by k tropically nonsingular submatrix, i.e. one whose tropical determinant has a unique minimizing permutation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume some m by k submatrix A of deg(M) has tropical rank less than k. By Develin-Santos-Sturmfels, cited as [4, Theorem 5.5], A has Kapranov rank less than k: there exists an m by k matrix A' over K with deg(A')=A and ordinary rank less than k. Choose a nonzero vector v in the kernel of A'. Then deg(v) satisfies all tropical hyperplane conditions determined by the rows of A, because each ordinary zero dot product forces the corresponding tropical minimum to be attained at least twice. Extend deg(v) to a vector w in T^n by putting ∞ in all coordinates outside the chosen k columns. This w lies in the tropical prevariety cut out by the rows of deg(M). Its finite support has size at most k. But since all Plücker coordinates of the (n−k)-dimensional L are nonzero, every point of deg(L) has support size at least k+1. Therefore w is not in deg(L), contradicting that the rows form a tropical basis. Hence each k-column submatrix has tropical rank k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the notation of Section 5, let L be a linear subspace over the Puiseux field K, and call a cocircuit of L a nonzero vector of L whose support is inclusion-minimal among nonzero vectors of L. Every nonzero v in L can be written as a finite sum v=v_1+...+v_d of cocircuits v_i in L, as stated in the paper, such that deg(v)=deg(v_1)⊕...⊕deg(v_d), i.e. coordinatewise deg(v)_j=min_i deg(v_i)_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v is already a cocircuit, there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, by definition of not being support-minimal, choose a cocircuit u with supp(u) strictly contained in supp(v). Multiply u by a scalar c of sufficiently large degree so that deg(cu)≥deg(v) coordinatewise. Choose c so that cu and v actually coincide in at least one coordinate, forcing supp(v−cu) to be strictly smaller than supp(v). Put v_1=cu. Since deg(v_1)≥deg(v), the nonarchimedean degree relation gives deg(v)=deg(v_1)⊕deg(v−v_1). If v−v_1 is not a cocircuit, repeat the same construction. The support strictly decreases at each step, so the process terminates with cocircuit summands satisfying the required tropical minimum identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be an n by d matrix over K whose column space is a linear subspace L⊂K^n. Let deg(A):T^d→T^n be the tropical linear map given by tropical matrix multiplication, so its image is the tropical span of the degree vectors of the columns of A. Then deg(L)=im(deg(A)) if and only if every cocircuit of L is represented in A, where a cocircuit is represented if its one-dimensional cocircuit class, equivalently its minimal support vector up to nonzero scalar, occurs among the columns of A. For any A one always has deg(L)⊇im(deg(A)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The general containment holds because every column of deg(A) is the degree vector of an element of L, and tropical linear spaces are closed under tropical linear combinations. For the if direction, take any nonzero v in L. By Lemma 15, v is a sum of cocircuits v_i with deg(v)=⊕_i deg(v_i). If every cocircuit is represented among the columns of A up to scalar, each deg(v_i) is a tropical scalar multiple of a column of deg(A). Hence deg(v) is in the tropical image of deg(A), giving deg(L)⊂im(deg(A)); together with the general containment this is equality. For the only-if direction, suppose a cocircuit c of L is not represented by A. Then deg(c) lies in deg(L). But any tropical linear combination of columns of deg(A) that is finite on all coordinates of supp(c) must use columns with finite entries there. Since no column has support contained in supp(c) unless it represents c, such columns also have finite coordinates outside supp(c); tropical minima cannot cancel those finite outside coordinates. Thus no vector in im(deg(A)) has exactly the finite support of deg(c), so deg(c) is not in im(deg(A)). This contradicts equality. The paper also notes that two cocircuits with the same support are scalar multiples, since otherwise a linear combination could cancel a coordinate and produce a smaller support.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit-hyperplane encoding of tropical linear spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper consistently replaces a tropical linear space by the intersection of tropical hyperplanes attached to its circuits. In the constant-coefficient case this becomes purely combinatorial: for each matroid circuit C, one imposes that the minimum of the coordinates indexed by C is attained at least twice. A smaller set of circuits is a tropical basis exactly when it excludes the same points as all circuits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Inclusion among tropical linear spaces in a relative Dressian can be attacked by comparing which circuit minimum conditions are imposed or implied. For subspaces such as Trop(M/F⊕U_{0,F}), one can try to write explicit circuit conditions and test how an order-reversing candidate would transform them.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction to 0/1 witnesses and flats
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3 turns any extra tropical point satisfying a proposed circuit basis into a 0/1 extra point by thresholding at the unique minimum of a violated circuit. Combined with the Ardila-Klivans flat criterion, this identifies the 0/1 points of a Bergman fan exactly with flats of the matroid. Thus tropical-basis equality can be checked by whether all nonflat supports are excluded.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Because the original problem starts with an involution on the lattice of flats, this technique gives a bridge from tropical containment questions back to flat combinatorics. It suggests testing any proposed involution on Dr(M) first on 0/1 strata or flat-supported points.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit pasting to generate tropical conditions
proof_techniques.description: If two circuits share a unique element and their symmetric difference is again a circuit, then the tropical condition for the symmetric difference follows from the two original conditions. The proof is a direct case analysis on whether the shared element participates in the repeated minima. Iterating this gives a way to prove that a small circuit set is a tropical basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a matroid with a flat-lattice duality, one can look for circuit or cocircuit generating sets stable under the duality and use pasting to reduce verification of containment or equality to those generators.

proof_techniques.name: Unique 0/1 witnesses for necessity of basis elements
proof_techniques.description: In the graphic, cographic, uniform, Fano, and R10 examples, the paper proves that certain circuits are unavoidable by constructing a 0/1 weight vector excluded by that circuit and by no other relevant circuit. This gives uniqueness or minimality of tropical bases in special classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Such witness constructions can reveal rigid elements of Dr(M): if a tropical subspace or constraint is uniquely characterized by a 0/1 witness, any order-reversing involution extending a flat involution may be forced to send it to a correspondingly rigid dual object.

proof_techniques.name: Local reduction via matroidal subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls that tropical Plücker vectors are equivalent to matroidal subdivisions of the hypersimplex and that the tropical linear space is the dual subcomplex supported on loop-free matroid polytopes. Since each cell is a matroid polytope, the star of any face of a tropical linear space is a constant-coefficient tropical linear space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to localize questions about arbitrary tropical linear spaces in Dr(M) to Bergman fans of matroids. A proposed global order-reversing map could be tested on local stars, where flat lattices, circuits, and cocircuits are available.

proof_techniques.name: Cocircuit tropical parametrization
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable linear space L over the Puiseux field, the paper studies when deg(L) is the tropical image of a matrix A whose columns span L. The answer is exactly that all cocircuits of L must be represented among the columns. The proof rests on decomposing every vector of L into cocircuits in a way compatible with coordinatewise tropical minima.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a dual presentation of realizable tropical linear spaces: circuits cut them out as intersections, while cocircuits can generate them as tropical spans. Since the original problem asks for an order-reversing extension of a flat duality, this circuit/cocircuit exchange is a natural mechanism to investigate.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical rank obstruction for tropical bases
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 12 gives a necessary condition for a set of linear forms to be a tropical basis of a realizable tropical linear space with all Plücker coordinates finite: every choice of k columns in the degree matrix of the defining forms must have tropical rank k, where k is the codimension. The proof constructs a low-support point in the tropical prevariety from a Kapranov-rank defect.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When testing candidate images of realizable tropical subspaces under a proposed involution, this rank condition can rule out proposed circuit descriptions that cannot define the intended tropical linear space.

proof_techniques.name: Direct-sum decomposition of tropical bases
proof_techniques.description: The direct sum of matroids has circuit set equal to the union of the circuit sets of the summands, and tropical bases decompose accordingly. This lets one analyze tropical basis questions componentwise.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural embedding of flats in the original problem uses matroids of the form M/F⊕U_{0,F}. The direct-sum lemma suggests decomposing their circuit and Bergman-fan descriptions into quotient and loop components.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Ardila-Klivans for the fact that the intersection of a Bergman fan with a sphere is a geometric realization of the order complex of the lattice of flats; it also notes that deleting loops and replacing each parallel class by one element does not change the lattice of flats. The paper raises Problem 7: what happens to tropical bases under minors, deletion and contraction; this is directly relevant to constructions involving M/F. Conjecture 13 states that the converse of Theorem 12 should hold when all Plücker coordinates are nonzero. Example 14 shows that the finite-Plücker-coordinate hypothesis in Theorem 12 is essential: for the 2-dimensional subspace of K^4 that is the kernel of [[1,0,1,1],[0,1,1,1],[1,-1,0,0]], the degree matrix [[0,∞,0,0],[∞,0,0,0],[0,0,∞,∞]] has rows forming a tropical basis even though its last two columns have tropical rank only 1; moreover the first two rows have the same tropical ranks on every subset of columns as the whole matrix but do not form a tropical basis, so tropical ranks alone do not determine tropical bases when some Plücker coordinates are ∞. The paper records further examples: for partition matroids, a minimal tropical basis is given by enough pairs in each block to form a spanning tree on that block; for the Fano matroid, the unique minimal tropical basis consists of the seven 3-element line circuits, with the 4-element circuits obtained by pasting; and it asks whether all regular matroids have unique minimal tropical bases. The cocircuit parametrization theorem is noted to specialize in the constant-coefficient case to Develin-Santos-Sturmfels [4, Proposition 7.5].
why_relevant: Gives circuit-based descriptions of tropical linear spaces. Circuit and cocircuit descriptions are often the most effective way to test containment, duality, and quotient conditions.
